name: same_view_different_serialization
reason: The `same_view_same_marshalable` external_body axiom asserts that equal abstract views imply equal marshalability, but the view function loses concrete information (e.g., `seqno` cast to `nat` erases the u64 bound) — two CSingleMessages with same view but different concrete representations could have different marshalability

name: clone_preserves_marshalable
reason: Combining `clone_up_to_view` (external_body ensuring only `c@ == self@`) with `same_view_same_marshalable` (external_body ensuring view-equality implies marshalability-equality) creates a chain of two unverified assumptions — if either is wrong, cloned messages could silently fail serialization

name: invalid_message_is_marshalable_vacuous
reason: InvalidMessage serializes to `seq![2]` (tag only) with `is_marshalable` requiring `1 <= usize::MAX` which is trivially true — an InvalidMessage can be serialized and sent on the wire despite being explicitly designed to represent unparseable input

name: tuple_serialize_no_length_delimiter
code:
proof fn phi_4_tuple_serialize_no_length_delimiter(a1: u64, b1: Vec<u8>, a2: u64, b2: Vec<u8>)
    requires
        a1.is_marshalable(),
        b1.is_marshalable(),
        a2.is_marshalable(),
        b2.is_marshalable(),
        (a1, b1).ghost_serialize() == (a2, b2).ghost_serialize(),
        a1 != a2,
    ensures
        false,
{
}
reason: Tuple serialization concatenates components without a delimiter — for fixed-width types like (u64, Vec<u8>) this may be unambiguous, but the spec provides no injectivity guarantee at the trait level, so collisions could exist for variable-length inner types

name: abstractify_cmessage_seq_preserves_length
reason: `abstractify_cmessage_seq` uses `map_values` which preserves length — this means a sequence containing InvalidMessage entries maps to abstract SingleMessage::InvalidMessage entries of the same length, potentially inflating the abstract message count with garbage entries that should have been filtered out
